topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot rattled, a familiar, mundane sound, but today it held something entirely unexpected. Tucked amongst the junk flyers and utility bills was a thick, cream-colored card, entirely blank save for a single, elegant script that read, "Happy Birthday." My hand, which had been reaching for the junk mail, froze mid-air. I pulled it out, the paper feeling impossibly delicate. My heart gave a sudden, heavy thump against my ribs. Who had done this? It wasn't addressed, no name, no signature. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over me, making my palms instantly slick. I quickly shoved the card back into the box, my movements jerky, trying to pretend I hadn't noticed the small, unsolicited gesture. I spent the next hour staring at the wall, the silence of my apartment amplifying the frantic buzzing in my ears.

[story 2]

I picked up the envelope, the paper cool beneath my fingertips. Inside, the card was pristine, a stark white rectangle bearing only the words: "Wishing you a wonderful day." A cold prickle ran down my spine. I stared at the message, my breath catching in my throat. It was clearly meant for me, but there was no clue as to the sender. A flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks a deep crimson. I felt a desperate urge to throw the whole thing into the nearest trash receptacle, to erase the intrusion. But my fingers remained glued to the card. I reread the simple message three times, a tight knot forming in my stomach. I couldn't bring myself to look for a return address, trapped in a loop of intense self-consciousness about this anonymous acknowledgment.

[story 3]

The sound of the mailbox opening was jarring. There it was, the card—unsealed, sitting there like an accidental artifact. My entire body tensed. I snatched it up, my grip surprisingly tight, and immediately felt a profound sense of exposure. It was a birthday card, clearly intended for me, yet utterly devoid of any identifying mark. My gaze darted around the hallway, as if expecting someone to materialize and demand an explanation. A strange tightness constricted my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I unfolded the card, the smooth paper offering no comfort. I quickly crumpled it into a tight ball, stuffing it deep into my pocket, desperate to hide the evidence of this bizarre, uninvited attention.
